attributes.stem:
A 16-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about an area of darker skin on his right shoulder that has gradually appeared over the past year. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. His mother has had a melanoma excised and his grandfather has had multiple basal cell carcinomas removed. His brother has seizures.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is a large, unilateral, brown patch with irregular borders on his right shoulder and upper chest. The patch has coarse dark hairs growing within it. The surrounding skin appears normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of Becker's naevus (Becker melanosis or pigmentary hamartoma).

Becker's naevus typically appears during adolescence as a unilateral, irregular, hyperpigmented patch with hypertrichosis (increased hair growth). It may also be hairless and darken over time, and may have acne or be thickened. It most commonly affects males and appears on the shoulder, upper trunk, or scapular area and crosses Blaschko's lines. The gradual onset and characteristic appearance in an adolescent male are highly suggestive of this diagnosis. Rarely it can be related to Becker naevus syndrome leading to change in the underlying tissues, however it is typically benign.

Café au lait macules are uniformly pigmented brown patches that are typically present from birth or early childhood. They do not demonstrate hypertrichosis and are usually multiple when associated with neurofibromatosis type 1.

Congenital melanocytic naevi are present at birth or appear within the first few months of life. They often have a more varied appearance with different shades of brown and black, and may have a raised or irregular surface.

Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation occurs following inflammation or injury to the skin. It would typically have a preceding history of trauma, inflammation, or skin condition, and would not demonstrate hypertrichosis.

Smooth muscle hamartoma presents as a circumscribed, flesh-colored or light brown patch that may demonstrate transient elevation or induration with rubbing (pseudo-Darier sign). While it can be associated with hypertrichosis, it typically presents at birth or in early infancy rather than adolescence.

Becker's naevus is a benign condition that requires no treatment unless desired for cosmetic reasons. Treatment options include laser hair removal for the hypertrichosis and various pigment-targeting lasers for the hyperpigmentation, though results can be variable.
learningPoints: Becker's naevus typically presents as a unilateral hyperpigmented patch with hypertrichosis in adolescent males.
The shoulder and upper trunk are the most common locations for Becker's naevus.
references: Dwivedi N, Mitchell G. Becker naevus.  DermNet NZ. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/becker-naevus

Litaiem N, Chabbouh A, Chabchoub I, Ben Naceur R, Mokni M, Zeglaoui F. Becker Nevus Syndrome: A Rare Entity but Important to Recognize. J Clin Aesthet Dermatol. 2024.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10941854/
